name: Qatar
tactical_document:
# Qatar — Tactical Profile

## Identity & Philosophy
Qatar are the **back-to-back AFC Asian Cup champions (2019, 2024)** and the most technically cohesive side in Asia, built on a single Aspire Academy generation that has grown up playing together. They are coached by **Julen Lopetegui**, who arrived wanting a proactive, possession-based 4-3-3 — but the reality of Group B has forced a pragmatic reset. Qatar opened with a creditable **1-1 draw vs Switzerland** (Khoukhi's last-gasp header earned their first-ever World Cup point) and were then **demolished 6-0 by Canada**, a match in which they were reduced to **nine men** (Homam Elamin sent off on 33', Assim Madibo on 53' for a horror tackle that broke Koné's leg). They sit on **1 point** going into the Group B finale, **Bosnia and Herzegovina v Qatar (Wed June 24, Lumen Field, Seattle)** — effectively a knockout: the winner keeps alive a slim best-third-placed hope, the loser is out. Both red cards bring **suspensions for Madibo and Homam Elamin**, gutting the midfield and a full-back slot, so Lopetegui reshuffles and recalls his veteran talisman **Almoez Ali** to lead the line for the first time this tournament. Qatar are heavy **underdogs (+ odds)** and will set up **compact and reactive**: defend deep, stay disciplined (after the Canada implosion), and strike on the counter through **Akram Afif**.

## Formation
- Shape: **4-3-3** on paper, but functionally a **compact 4-5-1 / 4-1-4-1 mid-to-low block** out of possession — the wide forwards (Afif left, Edmilson right) drop to form a midfield five, Almoez leads the line alone, and Qatar springs forward on transitions. In settled possession it loosens back to a 4-3-3 with Afif drifting inside.
- Role mapping (roster order in `qatar.yaml`):
  - index 0: GK — **Mahmoud Abunada** (#1) — Lopetegui's first-choice over Barsham; a 26-year-old reflex shot-stopper who has started both group games. Dominates his box; less of a sweeper, so the deep block must protect the space in behind.
  - index 1: LB — **Sultan Al-Brake** (#18) — left-back; hard-running, but in this game stays disciplined and narrow, only overlapping selectively on the counter so Afif can drift inside.
  - index 2: LCB — **Boualem Khoukhi** (#16) — veteran centre-back, aerial duel-winner, the organiser of the line and a genuine set-piece threat (scored the equaliser vs Switzerland).
  - index 3: RCB — **Pedro Miguel** (#2) — experienced near-100-cap defender, here at right-of-centre; disciplined, reads danger, covers for the overlapping full-back.
  - index 4: RB — **Ayoub Al-Oui** (#13) — Al-Gharafa right-back; comes in for the suspended Homam Elamin. Solid, defence-first full-back who tucks in to keep the back four narrow.
  - index 5: LCM — **Issa Laye** (#4) — Senegalese-born CB/DM playing as a left-of-centre midfielder; physical (strength 14, stamina 15), a ball-winner and screener covering the space Afif vacates.
  - index 6: DM — **Jassem Gaber** (#5) — Al-Rayyan CB/DM who anchors the midfield in place of the suspended Madibo; the disciplined holder who shields the back four and recycles possession.
  - index 7: RCM — **Ahmed Fathi** (#20) — Al-Arabi central midfielder; the more advanced of the three, carries and links to the front line, arrives late into the right half-space.
  - index 8: LW — **Akram Afif** (#11) — the star, 2023 Asian Cup Golden Ball winner and two-time Asian Player of the Year, Qatar's record creator (39 international goals, 125 caps). Plays from the left, cutting inside onto his right into the #10 space to receive between the lines. Skill 16, dribbling 16, shoot 15 — Qatar's one outlet that turns defence into a goal.
  - index 9: CF — **Almoez Ali** (#19) — Qatar's all-time leading scorer (55 goals in 115 caps). Recalled to start the must-win finale. The poacher and target man who runs the channels, holds up the long ball, attacks crosses, and finishes the chances Afif creates. Shoot 16, strength 14, penalty 16.
  - index 10: RW — **Edmilson Junior** (#8) — naturalised Belgian-born wide forward; quick and direct (speed 15, dribbling 15), the secondary counter outlet who comes inside to combine and links midfield to attack. Pass 14.

## Style of Play

### Build-up
Pragmatic and risk-averse against superior opposition. Qatar build from the back only when unpressed; under pressure they go long to **Almoez** to run the channel or hold up for Afif and Edmilson. When they do play out, the centre-backs Khoukhi and Pedro Miguel split, **Gaber** drops as a screen, and the full-backs stay relatively low. The chief progressor is whoever can find **Afif between the lines** — when he receives facing forward in space, Qatar is dangerous; everything routes through him. After the nine-man Canada collapse, ball security and **discipline are non-negotiable** — no needless fouls, no rash tackles.

### Pressing
**Mid-to-low block, selective triggers only.** Qatar does not chase; it stays compact in a 4-5-1 around or behind the halfway line and presses only on clear triggers: a heavy touch by a Bosnia defender, a back-pass under duress, or a loose ball in the wide channel. Almoez leads the front press; Afif and Edmilson curve their runs to shut the wide build-up; Laye and Fathi jump from midfield while Gaber holds. Against a stronger, more physical Bosnia side, Qatar prioritizes **compact numerical superiority** over high pressing — keep the shape, deny the centre, force play wide.

### Defensive shape
Compact **4-5-1** with the wide forwards (Afif left, Edmilson right) dropping to form a midfield five alongside Laye–Gaber–Fathi, and Almoez leading the line alone. The back four stays **narrow and deep**; the full-backs (Al-Brake, Al-Oui) tuck in to deny half-space runs and resist the urge to overlap. This block is the team's hidden weapon — the Aspire generation conceded only 1 goal in 6 matches at the 2023 Asian Cup, and the Switzerland draw showed the discipline is still there. Afif rarely tracks back hard, so Al-Brake and Laye must cover behind him. **Crucial after the Canada disaster: stay on your feet, no second yellow, no studs-up challenges.**

### Wide play
With the full-backs reined in defensively, width in attack comes mostly on **transitions**: when Qatar wins the ball, Al-Brake or Al-Oui can break forward outside the inverted wide forwards. In settled play, **Afif drifts inside from the left and Edmilson comes inside from the right** to combine, with Almoez attacking the near post and penalty spot. Qatar can switch play with long diagonals from Khoukhi to the opposite flank to relieve pressure.

### Final third
Patterns are **counter-led**: win the ball, feed **Afif** in the left half-space, let him dribble inside and shoot or slip a through-ball to **Almoez** running the channel. Edmilson's direct carry on the right. Almoez's poacher instinct in the box turns half-chances into goals — he is the man Qatar need to take their one or two clear looks. Afif's signature move: receive on the half-turn, dribble past two defenders, finish low to the far corner. In a must-win game late on, Qatar will throw bodies forward and trust Afif and Almoez to manufacture something.

## Set Pieces
- Attacking corners: **Afif** in-swingers from the right (left foot), **Edmilson** out-swingers from the left. Targets: Almoez (penalty spot, primary aerial), Khoukhi (back post — proven scorer), Pedro Miguel (near post flick-on). Set pieces are a vital route to goal for an underdog.
- Defending corners: hybrid — Khoukhi attacks the first ball; Almoez and Laye mark the two most dangerous opposition runners; zonal markers across the six-yard line; Abunada on his line. **Bosnia have aerial threats — concentration on the second ball is key.**
- Free kicks: **Afif** direct from any angle within 28 yards.
- Penalties: **Afif** primary, **Almoez** secondary (penalty 16), **Edmilson** tertiary.

## decide() Decision Priorities
1. **If my player_id ends with "_8" (LW Afif, #11) and team_phase == "attacking":** Drift inside from the left into the #10 space between the opposition lines, 8-12 units behind the index-9 Almoez. Receive on the half-turn.
2. **If my player_id ends with "_8" (LW Afif, #11) and I have the ball facing forward in the opposition half:** Dribble — skill 16, dribbling 16. Take on the first defender; Shoot if angle opens within 24 units. Afif is the primary attacking outlet.
3. **If my role == "GK" (index 0, Mahmoud Abunada, #1) and a cross is incoming:** Punch under physical pressure; otherwise stay on the line and protect the deep block.
4. **If my player_id ends with "_1" (LB Al-Brake, #18) or "_4" (RB Al-Oui, #13) and team_phase == "defending":** Tuck inside and stay narrow next to the CBs to deny the half-spaces; keep a compact back four. Do NOT push high — defensive discipline first.
5. **If my player_id ends with "_1" (LB Al-Brake) or "_4" (RB Al-Oui) and team_phase == "transition_attack":** Only then break forward to provide width outside the inverted wide forwards (index-8 Afif, index-10 Edmilson).
6. **If my player_id ends with "_6" (DM Gaber, #5) and the opposition has the ball within 35 units of my goal:** Step to screen in front of the back four and stay on your feet — NO reckless tackle (Qatar already lost two players to red cards). Show the carrier wide; only Tackle if it is a safe, side-on win.
7. **If team_phase == "transition_attack":** Index-8 Afif sprints into the left half-space to receive; index-9 Almoez runs the channel behind the CBs; index-10 Edmilson breaks on the right. Counter fast — this is Qatar's best route to goal.
8. **If my player_id ends with "_9" (CF Almoez, #19) and the index-8 Afif has the ball:** Make a channel-run behind the opposition CBs OR pin the last defender and attack the cut-back / near post. Almoez is the finisher Qatar must feed.
9. **If my player_id ends with "_9" (CF Almoez) and a long ball is played forward under pressure:** Hold up play (strength 14), shield the ball, and lay it off to the arriving index-8 Afif or index-10 Edmilson.
10. **If team is trailing in the final 20 minutes (must-win — a draw or loss likely eliminates Qatar):** Abandon the low block, push to a front-heavy shape: index-10 Edmilson and index-8 Afif stay high and wide, index-7 Fathi pushes up behind index-9 Almoez, full-backs overload the flanks, Khoukhi joins set-piece deliveries into the box.
11. **If a defensive corner is incoming:** The index-2 Khoukhi marks the most dangerous opposition striker; the index-5 Laye marks the second threat; index-9 Almoez and index-8 Afif stay forward as counter-outlets.
12. **Set-pieces 20-28 yards from goal:** Defer dead-ball to the index-8 Afif (left-footed in-swingers, direct free kicks); penalties to Afif, then index-9 Almoez.

## Key Player Notes
- **Afif (8):** The 2023 Asian Cup Golden Ball winner and two-time Asian Player of the Year; Qatar's record creator (39 international goals). The inverted left winger who drifts between the lines — the team's only world-class technical player and its single decisive outlet on the counter.
- **Almoez (9):** Qatar's all-time leading scorer (55 goals in 115 caps). Recalled to start the must-win finale after coming off the bench in the first two games. The focal-point striker — poacher, channel-runner, hold-up man, aerial threat. Shoot 16, strength 14, penalty 16.
- **Edmilson Junior (10):** The naturalised Belgian-born winger; quick, direct, the secondary counter outlet. Speed 15, dribbling 15, pass 14.
- **Khoukhi (2):** The veteran CB. Aerial duel-winner, line organiser, and set-piece threat at both ends — scored the historic equaliser vs Switzerland.
- **Gaber (6):** The midfield anchor, in for the suspended Madibo. CB/DM by trade; shields the back four and recycles possession — must be disciplined, no rash challenges.
- **Laye (5):** The physical ball-winning LCM (in for the gutted midfield); covers the space Afif leaves behind.
- **Al-Oui (4):** The RB deputising for the suspended Homam Elamin; defence-first, stays narrow.

## Tournament Mindset
Qatar arrive at the Group B finale **on 1 point, eliminated if they lose**, carrying the scars of a **6-0, nine-man humiliation by Canada**. The 1-1 vs Switzerland proved the **compact, disciplined version of this team can frustrate better sides** — that is the blueprint for Bosnia. The mentality is pragmatic: **stay tight, stay on your feet (no more red cards), survive, and steal the game through an Afif transition, an Almoez finish, or a Khoukhi set-piece header.** Qatar are heavy underdogs and know it; their hope rests on tournament composure, Aspire-trained defensive cohesion, and the two players capable of a moment of magic — Afif and Almoez. The vulnerability is **goal-scoring depth and physicality against a more powerful Bosnia side**: if the two stars are marked out, the secondary creators (Edmilson, Fathi) must step up. For Qatar, this is win-or-go-home — defend like the Switzerland game, attack like they have nothing to lose.
